title: Setup Python Environment and Dependencies
description: Create isolated environment and install all required packages for SAE training, robustness evaluation, and model inference.

steps.step1: Create virtual environment: python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
steps.step2: Install PyTorch 2.1+ with CUDA: pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121
steps.step3: Install transformers: pip install transformers==4.36.0 accelerate
steps.step4: Install SAE-related packages: pip install sae-lens transformer-lens
steps.step5: Install evaluation packages: pip install scipy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn pandas numpy
steps.step6: Install utility packages: pip install tqdm wandb einops
steps.step7: Verify GPU availability: python -c 'import torch; print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))'
steps.step8: Create directory structure: mkdir -p data models results figures logs

title: Download and Prepare Datasets
description: Download training and evaluation datasets. Prepare activation extraction pipeline.

steps.step1: Download OpenWebText subset: Use HuggingFace datasets to load 2M tokens (approx 5000 sequences of 512 tokens)
steps.step2: Create train/val split: 90% train (4500 sequences), 10% validation (500 sequences)
steps.step3: Prepare Art & Science dataset: Load from Li et al. (2025) robustness evaluation benchmark - use their released prompts for feature activation attacks
steps.step4: Prepare AG News: Load 1000 samples for domain generalization testing (500 train, 500 test)
steps.step5: Cache tokenized datasets: Save as .pt files for fast loading during training
steps.step6: Verify dataset statistics: Log sequence lengths, token distributions, and dataset sizes
steps.step7: Create semantic perturbation cache: Generate paraphrased variants for 1000 validation samples using a small LLM (e.g., GPT-2) for consistency training

title: Extract LLM Activations
description: Pre-extract activations from target layers to speed up SAE training.

steps.step1: Load Pythia-70M model: From EleutherAI/pythia-70m-deduped using TransformerLens
steps.step2: Extract layer 2 activations (middle layer): Shape [batch, seq_len, d_model=512]
steps.step3: Extract activations for all training data: Store as memmap or chunked .pt files
steps.step4: Repeat for validation set
steps.step5: Verify activation statistics: Mean, std, check for NaNs or outliers

title: Train Standard TopK SAE Baseline
description: Train vanilla TopK SAE as primary baseline for comparison. This is the simplest and most common baseline.

steps.step1: Set hyperparameters: d_model=512, expansion_factor=4 (d_sae=2048), topk=32, l1_coeff=0
steps.step2: Use 3 random seeds: 42, 43, 44 for statistical significance
steps.step3: Training config: batch_size=4096 tokens, learning_rate=3e-4, num_epochs=10
steps.step4: Optimizer: Adam with betas=(0.9, 0.999), no weight decay
steps.step5: Loss: MSE reconstruction loss only (standard TopK uses topk sparsity, no L1)
steps.step6: Log metrics every 1000 steps: FVU, L0 sparsity, alive features
steps.step7: Save checkpoints: Best by validation FVU, and final model

title: Train JumpReLU SAE Baseline
description: Train JumpReLU SAE as strong architectural baseline. JumpReLU uses threshold-based activation with learned thresholds.

steps.step1: Set hyperparameters: d_model=512, expansion_factor=4 (d_sae=2048), bandwidth=0.001
steps.step2: Use 3 random seeds: 42, 43, 44
steps.step3: Training config: batch_size=4096, lr=3e-4, num_epochs=10
steps.step4: JumpReLU architecture: Use ReLU with learned threshold parameters
steps.step5: Threshold loss: Add small penalty to encourage reasonable thresholds
steps.step6: Log same metrics as TopK baseline for fair comparison

title: Replicate Denoising SAE (Beyond Sparsity)
description: Implement and train the denoising SAE from Pan et al. (ICML 2025) for direct comparison. This addresses the feedback to compare directly with concurrent work.

steps.step1: Implement denoising: Add input dropout (dropout_rate=0.3) to activations during training
steps.step2: Architecture: Same as TopK (d_sae=2048, topk=32)
steps.step3: Training: Standard reconstruction loss with denoising objective
steps.step4: Key difference from RobustSAE: Uses RANDOM noise (dropout) for diversity, not semantic perturbations for robustness
steps.step5: Use 3 random seeds: 42, 43, 44
steps.step7: Log reconstruction quality and feature diversity metrics
steps.step9: This baseline tests: Does diversity training (Pan et al.) improve robustness? (Expected: diversity helps coverage, not adversarial robustness)

title: Train RobustSAE Full Method
description: Train RobustSAE with consistency regularization and unsupervised robustness proxy. This is the main proposed method.

steps.step1: Set hyperparameters: d_model=512, d_sae=2048, topk=32
steps.step2: Consistency loss weight: lambda_consist=0.1 (selected from {0.01, 0.1, 1.0} based on preliminary validation)
steps.step3: Use 3 random seeds: 42, 43, 44
steps.step4: Training config: batch_size=2048 (reduced due to double forward pass), lr=3e-4, num_epochs=10
steps.step5: Generate semantic perturbations: For each batch, create perturbed variants using: (a) token synonym substitution, (b) minor punctuation changes
steps.step7: Total loss: MSE_recon + lambda_sparse * L1_sparse + lambda_consist * L_consistency
steps.step8: Compute unsupervised robustness proxy during training: Gradient concentration score R_i for each feature
steps.step9: Log: FVU, L0 sparsity, consistency loss value, proxy scores distribution

title: Ablation: RobustSAE without Proxy (Consistency Only)
description: Test contribution of unsupervised robustness proxy by training with consistency loss but without proxy-based feature weighting.

steps.step1: Same architecture and training as Full RobustSAE
steps.step2: Ablate: Do NOT compute or use gradient concentration proxy scores
steps.step3: Purpose: Isolate the effect of the unsupervised proxy component
steps.step4: Use 3 random seeds: 42, 43, 44
steps.step5: Training config: Same as Full Method (batch_size=2048, lr=3e-4, num_epochs=10)
steps.step7: Compare: Full vs No-Proxy to measure proxy contribution to robustness

title: Ablation: Varying Consistency Loss Weight
description: Test sensitivity to lambda_consist hyperparameter. Understand reconstruction-robustness tradeoff.

steps.step1: Fix seed=42 for controlled comparison
steps.step2: Train with lambda_consist in {0.0 (baseline), 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}
steps.step3: Keep all other hyperparameters identical
steps.step4: Record: FVU, L0 sparsity, training time per epoch
steps.step5: Output: Tradeoff curve showing reconstruction vs robustness as function of lambda_consist

title: Ablation: Different Perturbation Types
description: Compare semantic perturbations vs random dropout for the consistency objective.

steps.step1: Condition A: Semantic perturbations (paraphrases, synonyms) - same as main experiment
steps.step2: Condition B: Random dropout (noise=0.3) - similar to Pan et al. denoising
steps.step3: Condition C: Adversarial perturbations (lightweight PGD on activations)
steps.step4: Fix seed=42, lambda_consist=0.1
steps.step5: Train 3 models, compare: reconstruction quality, robustness metrics
steps.step6: Key question: Do semantic perturbations provide better robustness than random noise?

title: Robustness Evaluation: Population-Level Attacks
description: Evaluate all trained models against population-level activation attacks following Li et al. (2025).

steps.step1: Load Art & Science dataset with 2 semantic categories: art and science
steps.step2: For each model, compute baseline feature activations on both categories
steps.step3: Implement population attack: Find suffix that, when appended to art prompts, makes SAE activations look like science prompts
steps.step4: Attack method: Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) adapted for SAE activations
steps.step5: Metric: Overlap change - Jaccard similarity between top-activating features before and after attack
steps.step6: Compute for all models and all seeds
steps.step7: Statistical test: Paired t-test between RobustSAE and each baseline

title: Robustness Evaluation: Individual Feature Attacks
description: Test if individual features can be adversarially activated/manipulated.

steps.step1: Select 50 high-activating features per model (most interpretable ones)
steps.step2: For each target feature, run activation attack: Find input perturbation that activates target feature
steps.step3: Attack method: Gradient-based token optimization (simplified GCG)
steps.step4: Metric: Attack Success Rate (ASR) - % of features successfully activated with perturbation budget of 10 tokens
steps.step5: Also measure: Average number of iterations to successful attack
steps.step7: Statistical significance: Chi-square test for ASR differences

title: Direct Comparison with Denoising SAE
description: Head-to-head comparison addressing the feedback: establish clear differentiation from Pan et al. (ICML 2025).

steps.step1: Compare RobustSAE vs Denoising SAE on: (a) Robustness metrics, (b) Diversity metrics
steps.step2: Robustness metrics: Population overlap change, individual ASR
steps.step3: Diversity metrics: Feature activation frequency entropy, pairwise feature correlation
steps.step4: Key claim: RobustSAE improves robustness without harming diversity; Denoising improves diversity without improving robustness
steps.step5: Create comparison table showing which method wins on which metric
steps.step6: Show that methods are complementary: Propose combined Robust+Diverse SAE

title: Validate Unsupervised Robustness Proxy
description: Test if the gradient concentration proxy correlates with actual adversarial vulnerability.

steps.step1: For RobustSAE trained models, extract proxy scores: R_i = ||grad_h z_i||_2 / (|z_i| + epsilon)
steps.step2: Get empirical robustness: For each feature i, measure how easily it can be adversarially activated (from individual attack results)
steps.step3: Compute Spearman correlation between proxy score R_i and empirical attack success rate
steps.step4: Also compute Pearson correlation and Kendall tau
steps.step5: Test hypothesis: Correlation > 0.6 indicates proxy is useful for identifying fragile features
steps.step6: Visualize: Scatter plot of proxy score vs attack success rate
steps.step7: Identify: Top 10% most fragile features by proxy - do they align with top 10% most attacked features?

title: Standard SAE Metrics Evaluation
description: Compute standard SAE quality metrics for all models.

steps.step1: Fraction of Variance Unexplained (FVU): MSE / Var(h) on validation set
steps.step2: L0 Sparsity: Average number of active features per token
steps.step3: Loss Recovered: When substituting SAE reconstructions back into LLM forward pass
steps.step4: Dead Features: Percentage of features never activated on validation set
steps.step5: Feature Density: Distribution of feature activation frequencies
steps.step6: Compute for all models, aggregate across seeds

title: Feature Steering Stability
description: Evaluate if feature steering vectors are more stable with RobustSAE.

steps.step1: Select 10 interpretable features (e.g., number features, punctuation features)
steps.step2: For each feature, create steering vector by scaling its decoder weight
steps.step3: Apply steering to base prompts and perturbed prompts
steps.step4: Metric: Steering effect consistency - correlation between steering effect on original vs perturbed inputs
steps.step5: Compare consistency across RobustSAE and baselines

title: Generate Paper Figures
description: Create publication-quality figures showing key results.

steps.step1: Figure 1: Method illustration - Diagram of RobustSAE architecture with consistency loss
steps.step2: Figure 2: Reconstruction-Robustness tradeoff - Scatter plot: FVU (x) vs Attack Success Rate (y) for all methods
steps.step3: Figure 3: Proxy validation - Scatter plot: Proxy score vs empirical attack success with regression line
steps.step4: Figure 4: Comparison with Denoising - Grouped bar chart showing RobustSAE wins on robustness, Denoising wins on diversity
steps.step5: Figure 5: Feature activation heatmap - Show stability of top features under perturbation
steps.step6: Save all figures to figures/ directory in PDF and PNG formats

title: Generate Results Tables
description: Create LaTeX-formatted tables for paper.

steps.step1: Table 1: Main results - All metrics for all methods (mean ± std over 3 seeds)
steps.step2: Table 2: Ablation study results - Component ablations with statistical significance
steps.step3: Table 3: Comparison with concurrent work - Direct comparison with Denoising SAE
steps.step4: Table 4: Proxy validation - Correlation coefficients and prediction accuracy
steps.step5: Save tables as LaTeX files in figures/tables.tex and CSV in results/tables.csv

title: Statistical Significance Testing
description: Rigorous statistical testing of all claims.

steps.step1: For each metric, perform paired t-test between RobustSAE and each baseline
steps.step2: Use Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (alpha=0.05/num_comparisons)
steps.step3: Report effect sizes (Cohen's d) for significant differences
steps.step4: Confidence intervals: 95% CI for all reported metrics
steps.step5: Verify success criteria: Attack success reduction >25% with p<0.01, proxy correlation >0.6
steps.step6: Save statistical test results as results/statistical_tests.json

title: Final Success Criteria Check
description: Evaluate whether experiments meet success criteria from proposal.

steps.step1: Check Confirmed: RobustSAE >25% reduction in attack success vs Standard TopK (p<0.01)?
steps.step2: Check Confirmed: FVU maintained within 10% of baseline?
steps.step3: Check Confirmed: Proxy correlation >0.6 Spearman?
steps.step4: Check Confirmed: Improvements generalize across attack types?
steps.step5: If criteria met: Document as 'Hypothesis Confirmed'
steps.step6: If not met: Document partial success or refutation with analysis
